bins.name: Jurisdictional Limits on FERC
bins.desc: PFT acknowledges FERC has some authority over transmission-level interconnection in RTO/ISO footprints but warns that DOE's proposal risks expanding that authority into retail service territory reserved to states. The filer stresses that loads and generators are fundamentally different and that the Federal Power Act does not authorize FERC to regulate the bundled retail services large loads seek. Federal action should stay within those limits.
quotes: Facilitating expeditious interconnection of these new customers is an important goal, but the proposal raises key questions about FERC's jurisdiction and, ultimately, could exacerbate the challenges of new large loads: speedy interconnection without expanding supply will strain reliability further and increase costs for all.

PFT respectfully urges the Commission to: (1) recognize the vital state regulatory role in effectively managing and serving large loads, overseeing intrastate transmission, and determining cost allocation; and (2) cautiously examine its jurisdictional authority so as to remain within the bounds of the law and preserve states' jurisdiction to continue to develop and deploy successful approaches to the large load challenge.

While large load interconnection presents challenges nationwide, the nature and severity of these challenges vary dramatically based on regional market structure and the effectiveness of state regulatory oversight.
Generators seek access to wholesale markets, within FERC's jurisdiction, but large loads seek to interconnect to obtain bundled retail services, within states' jurisdiction.

DOE's assertion that transmission service encompasses 'the appurtenant benefits of such' threatens state jurisdiction to the extent that new large loads could, for example, argue that failure to provide them energy and capacity is a violation of the terms of their interconnection or entitles them to direct access to wholesale markets.

FERC should avoid taking action or asserting jurisdiction in such a way that would serve as an effective preemption of large load interconnection through DOE Section 403 authority or sweeping changes to FERC-jurisdictional interconnection procedures could disrupt effective state regulation without corresponding benefits in jurisdictions where state processes work well.

DOE and FERC could consider, for example, establishing safe harbor provisions whereby states demonstrating effective management of large load interconnection remain free from federal procedural mandates or that encourage states to continue to holistically solve the interrelated challenges of interconnection of large loads and critical new generation.

bins.name: Speed Without Supply Backfires
bins.desc: PFT opposes framing expedited interconnection as an unqualified goal. Faster queue processing that outpaces new generation additions will tighten supply and raise costs for all customers, making the underlying problem worse rather than better.
quotes: Facilitating expeditious interconnection of these new customers is an important goal, but the proposal raises key questions about FERC's jurisdiction and, ultimately, could exacerbate the challenges of new large loads: speedy interconnection without expanding supply will strain reliability further and increase costs for all.

bins.name: Co-location Cost Avoidance
bins.desc: PFT is concerned that co-location arrangements allow large loads to bypass transmission upgrade costs and network charges, reducing the generation pool available to other market participants and raising costs for existing customers.
quotes: Such arrangements potentially avoid transmission upgrade costs and network service charges while creating reliability concerns: largest customers that can satisfy their demand close to the point of generation, effectively reducing the pool of generation available to all other customers participating in markets.

bins.name: Protect Existing Customer Costs
bins.desc: PFT consistently supports cost protections for existing ratepayers. The filer cites Georgia's risk-based billing rules for data centers as a model and urges FERC to ensure any federal action is paired with measures that prevent cost shifts from large loads onto ordinary customers.
quotes: Facilitating expeditious interconnection of these new customers is an important goal, but the proposal raises key questions about FERC's jurisdiction and, ultimately, could exacerbate the challenges of new large loads: speedy interconnection without expanding supply will strain reliability further and increase costs for all.

PFT respectfully urges the Commission to: (1) recognize the vital state regulatory role in effectively managing and serving large loads, overseeing intrastate transmission, and determining cost allocation; and (2) cautiously examine its jurisdictional authority so as to remain within the bounds of the law and preserve states' jurisdiction to continue to develop and deploy successful approaches to the large load challenge.

Such arrangements potentially avoid transmission upgrade costs and network service charges while creating reliability concerns: largest customers that can satisfy their demand close to the point of generation, effectively reducing the pool of generation available to all other customers participating in markets.

In January 2025, the Georgia PSC unanimously approved new rules requiring data centers using more than 100 MW to be billed based on project risks and to pay transmission and distribution costs as construction progresses.

PFT urges FERC to support the existing functions of state utility regulators while focusing on reforming capacity markets, strengthening transmission planning requirements, and taking a hard look at whether co-located loads are avoiding their share of grid costs and upgrades.

bins.name: Co-location Must Pay Grid Costs
bins.desc: PFT urges FERC to examine whether co-located loads are avoiding their fair share of grid upgrade costs. The DOE framing that bundles transmission interconnection with energy and capacity access could let co-located facilities argue entitlement to wholesale market services while escaping transmission charges.
quotes: Such arrangements potentially avoid transmission upgrade costs and network service charges while creating reliability concerns: largest customers that can satisfy their demand close to the point of generation, effectively reducing the pool of generation available to all other customers participating in markets.

DOE's assertion that transmission service encompasses 'the appurtenant benefits of such' threatens state jurisdiction to the extent that new large loads could, for example, argue that failure to provide them energy and capacity is a violation of the terms of their interconnection or entitles them to direct access to wholesale markets.

PFT urges FERC to support the existing functions of state utility regulators while focusing on reforming capacity markets, strengthening transmission planning requirements, and taking a hard look at whether co-located loads are avoiding their share of grid costs and upgrades.

bins.name: Planning and Disclosure Requirements
bins.desc: PFT supports requiring large loads to provide accurate, timely information on their plans and operations to support transmission and generation planning. The filer also endorses safe harbor provisions tied to demonstrated state-level planning effectiveness, and calls for FERC to strengthen transmission planning requirements broadly.
quotes: DOE and FERC could consider, for example, establishing safe harbor provisions whereby states demonstrating effective management of large load interconnection remain free from federal procedural mandates or that encourage states to continue to holistically solve the interrelated challenges of interconnection of large loads and critical new generation.

PFT urges FERC to support the existing functions of state utility regulators while focusing on reforming capacity markets, strengthening transmission planning requirements, and taking a hard look at whether co-located loads are avoiding their share of grid costs and upgrades.

FERC should at a minimum take steps to require that transmission-interconnected large loads participate in efforts to ensure the reliability of the grid and support good transmission and generation planning by providing accurate and timely information about their plans and operations—and are penalized for failure to disclose relevant information or for seeking multiple, duplicative interconnections that distort load forecasting and planning.

bins.name: Challenges Vary by Region
bins.desc: The filing documents that large load interconnection difficulties are concentrated in certain RTO and ISO footprints and are not a uniform national problem. Southeast vertically integrated utilities and MISO South have managed large load additions without the queue backlogs seen elsewhere, pointing to market structure as the key variable.
quotes: While large load interconnection presents challenges nationwide, the nature and severity of these challenges vary dramatically based on regional market structure and the effectiveness of state regulatory oversight.
Throughout the Southeast, vertically integrated utilities have successfully interconnected large loads without the queue backlogs and cost-shifting controversies that exist in some markets.

bins.name: State Oversight Delivers Results
bins.desc: PFT highlights Georgia PSC's 2025 data center billing rules and the broader Southeast track record as evidence that state commissions with certificate of need proceedings and integrated resource planning can handle large load interconnection effectively without federal mandates.
quotes: In January 2025, the Georgia PSC unanimously approved new rules requiring data centers using more than 100 MW to be billed based on project risks and to pay transmission and distribution costs as construction progresses.
Throughout the Southeast, vertically integrated utilities have successfully interconnected large loads without the queue backlogs and cost-shifting controversies that exist in some markets.

bins.name: Federal Preemption Risks Backfire
bins.desc: PFT warns that broad federal action under DOE Section 403 authority could disrupt ongoing state proceedings that are working well, delay projects currently moving through state processes, and impose ISO/RTO-style procedures on vertically integrated utilities where they do not fit.
quotes: FERC should avoid taking action or asserting jurisdiction in such a way that would serve as an effective preemption of large load interconnection through DOE Section 403 authority or sweeping changes to FERC-jurisdictional interconnection procedures could disrupt effective state regulation without corresponding benefits in jurisdictions where state processes work well.

bins.name: Safe Harbor for Effective States
bins.desc: PFT recommends that FERC and DOE create safe harbor provisions exempting states that have demonstrated effective large load management from new federal procedural requirements, preserving successful approaches while targeting intervention where problems are real.
quotes: DOE and FERC could consider, for example, establishing safe harbor provisions whereby states demonstrating effective management of large load interconnection remain free from federal procedural mandates or that encourage states to continue to holistically solve the interrelated challenges of interconnection of large loads and critical new generation.

bins.name: Penalize Duplicative Requests
bins.desc: PFT asks FERC to require large loads to disclose plans and operations to support grid planning and to penalize those that seek multiple duplicative interconnection requests that distort load forecasts.
quotes: FERC should at a minimum take steps to require that transmission-interconnected large loads participate in efforts to ensure the reliability of the grid and support good transmission and generation planning by providing accurate and timely information about their plans and operations—and are penalized for failure to disclose relevant information or for seeking multiple, duplicative interconnections that distort load forecasting and planning.
